text: City officials announced Monday that the new light-rail extension will open ahead of schedule on June 15. The project, originally slated for August, was accelerated after the contractor completed track-laying three weeks early. Mayor Alvarez called it 'a rare on-time, on-budget win' and credited federal infrastructure grants for closing the funding gap. Daily ridership on the line is projected to reach 42,000 within a year.

A magnitude 5.8 earthquake struck off the coast of northern Chile early Tuesday, the U.S. Geological Survey reported. No tsunami warning was issued. Power was knocked out in three coastal towns and at least 12 buildings sustained structural damage. Local officials said no fatalities had been reported as of midday.

Pharma giant Norvelt said Wednesday it will halt development of its experimental Alzheimer's drug after a Phase 3 trial showed no benefit over placebo. The company's stock fell 14% in pre-market trading. CEO Anita Roy said the firm would redirect resources toward two earlier-stage compounds. Analysts had expected the drug to generate $2 billion in annual sales by 2028.

Heavy rainfall has flooded parts of Bangkok for the third consecutive day, forcing authorities to evacuate roughly 8,000 residents from low-lying districts. The Meteorological Department predicts another 100 mm of rain by Friday. Schools in five districts are closed and several subway stations have suspended service. Officials are coordinating with the army to deploy additional pumps.

The European Central Bank held its main policy rate at 3.25% on Thursday, in line with economist forecasts. President Lagarde said the bank was 'not in a hurry' to cut rates further despite slowing inflation. Markets had priced in a 30% chance of a quarter-point cut. The euro strengthened modestly against the dollar following the decision.

A federal judge ruled Friday that the Department of Energy must release internal documents related to its 2023 grid-resilience grants. The ruling sided with a transparency nonprofit that had sued under the Freedom of Information Act. The agency had argued the documents contained sensitive infrastructure details. DOE has 30 days to comply or appeal.

Tech firm Lumen Robotics unveiled its first humanoid warehouse robot on Tuesday at a launch event in San Jose. The robot, called Pax-1, is priced at $48,000 and targets logistics customers. Lumen said it has signed pilot agreements with two Fortune-500 retailers but declined to name them. Competitor Figure AI launched a similar product last quarter.

Brazil's senate approved a sweeping tax reform bill late Wednesday by a vote of 53 to 22. The legislation consolidates five existing consumption taxes into a single value-added tax to be phased in over seven years. President Lula praised the bill as 'the most significant economic reform in three decades.' Critics warn the transition period will create accounting confusion for small businesses.

Researchers at MIT reported in Nature on Thursday that a new battery chemistry using sodium-ion electrodes achieved 92% capacity retention after 5,000 charge cycles. The team said the design avoids both lithium and cobalt, easing supply-chain concerns. Commercialization remains several years off, the lead author cautioned, citing manufacturing challenges. Several Chinese firms are pursuing parallel sodium-ion designs.

Wildfires in southern Greece have burned more than 28,000 hectares since igniting last weekend, civil protection authorities said. Two firefighters and one civilian have died. Greece has requested aerial firefighting support from France, Italy, and Spain under the EU's civil protection mechanism. High winds are expected to complicate containment efforts through the weekend.

The Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge, core PCE, rose 0.2% in April and 2.7% over the prior year, the Commerce Department reported. The figure matched consensus expectations. Personal spending grew 0.3%, slower than March's pace. Treasury yields ticked down on the release as traders interpreted the data as supportive of a September rate cut.

Indonesia's parliament passed a controversial new criminal code on Tuesday that prohibits cohabitation between unmarried couples and bans insults to the president. Rights groups condemned the law, which takes effect in 2026. Tourism officials downplayed concerns about foreign visitors, saying enforcement would require a formal complaint from a family member. The law also restricts certain political demonstrations.

Streaming service Vortex reported its first profitable quarter on Tuesday, posting net income of $87 million. Subscriber growth slowed to 1.2 million additions, well below the 2.4 million Wall Street expected. The company attributed the profit to a recent price hike and ad-tier expansion. Shares fell 6% after-hours on the subscriber miss.

Scientists at the Large Hadron Collider announced preliminary evidence of an unexpectedly heavy variant of the W boson, potentially in tension with the Standard Model. The result, presented at a Geneva conference, carries a statistical significance of 3.4 sigma — below the 5-sigma threshold for discovery. Other detector collaborations have not yet confirmed the finding. Theorists are already proposing extensions to accommodate the result.

Hurricane Ophelia strengthened to a Category 3 storm Friday as it approached the Gulf Coast. The National Hurricane Center forecasts landfall near Galveston, Texas, late Saturday night. Mandatory evacuation orders are in effect for three coastal counties. Oil refiners began shutting down platforms in the western Gulf as a precaution.

South Korea's central bank cut its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 3.00% on Thursday, citing weakening export demand. The decision was unanimous among the seven-member board. The bank also revised its 2026 GDP growth forecast down to 1.9% from 2.2%. The won fell roughly 0.4% against the dollar after the announcement.

A coalition of 14 state attorneys general filed an antitrust lawsuit against grocery delivery company Mercado, alleging it pressured retailers to set higher in-store prices to subsidize its delivery margins. Mercado denied the claims, calling the suit 'politically motivated.' The complaint seeks structural remedies including potential divestiture. The case was filed in the Northern District of California.

The World Health Organization declared the recent Marburg virus outbreak in Tanzania contained on Monday after 42 consecutive days without new cases. The outbreak, which began in late February, infected 19 people and killed 11. Health workers credited rapid contact tracing and an experimental vaccine deployed under WHO's emergency protocol. Surveillance will continue for another 90 days.

Apple is in advanced talks to acquire AI startup Perlin Labs for roughly $1.8 billion, according to two people familiar with the matter. The deal would mark Apple's largest AI-focused acquisition. Perlin, founded in 2022, specializes in on-device language models. Apple declined to comment; Perlin did not respond to requests for comment.

Climate negotiators wrapped up two weeks of talks in Geneva on Saturday with a draft agreement on methane reduction targets. The text commits signatories to cutting methane emissions 35% from 2020 levels by 2035. Major emitters India and Russia did not sign on. Environmental groups called the deal 'a meaningful step but far short of what the science demands.'

A coordinated cyberattack disrupted operations at three major European ports on Wednesday, slowing container processing at Rotterdam, Hamburg, and Antwerp. No ransom demand has been publicly disclosed. EU cybersecurity agency ENISA said preliminary analysis pointed to a state-affiliated actor but stopped short of attribution. Shipping schedules are expected to be disrupted for several days.

The Supreme Court agreed on Monday to hear a case challenging the constitutionality of social media content-moderation laws in Texas and Florida. Oral arguments are scheduled for the October term. The laws restrict platforms from removing political content based on viewpoint. Tech industry groups argue the laws violate the First Amendment.

Renewable energy generated 41% of U.S. electricity in the first quarter, the Energy Information Administration reported, surpassing both coal and nuclear. Solar grew 28% year-over-year, while wind generation declined slightly due to weaker average wind speeds. Natural gas remains the largest single source at 38%. The agency projected renewables would exceed natural gas by 2028.

Argentine President Milei announced the elimination of the country's wealth tax effective immediately on Friday, fulfilling a campaign promise. Critics said the move would cost the government roughly $4 billion in annual revenue. Milei argued it would spur capital repatriation and investment. The peso strengthened modestly on the news.

A pediatric ward fire at a public hospital in Karachi killed at least 14 children early Sunday, authorities said. An electrical short was identified as the preliminary cause. The hospital had failed two recent fire-safety inspections, according to local reports. Prime Minister Sharif ordered a federal investigation and pledged compensation to affected families.

Boeing reached a tentative agreement with its largest machinist union late Thursday, potentially ending a six-week strike. The deal includes a 38% wage increase over four years and a one-time $7,000 ratification bonus. Union members are expected to vote on the contract Monday. Boeing has lost an estimated $5 billion in revenue during the work stoppage.

A new study published in The Lancet found that adults who walk 8,000 steps even just one or two days per week had 14.9% lower all-cause mortality over a decade. The analysis covered 3,100 participants from a U.S. health survey. Researchers cautioned the design was observational and could not establish causation. The findings could simplify physical-activity guidelines, the authors suggested.

OPEC+ agreed Sunday to extend its current production cuts of 2.2 million barrels per day through the end of 2026. The decision was largely expected by markets. Saudi Arabia's energy minister said the alliance retained flexibility to adjust output. Crude prices were little changed Monday morning.

Australia's competition regulator approved Telstra's $1.2 billion acquisition of regional ISP Skyline Networks, with conditions requiring divestiture of overlapping fiber assets in two states. The deal had been under review for 11 months. Telstra said the conditions were 'workable' and expects to close the transaction by year-end. Industry analysts said the precedent could shape future telecom consolidation.

A new exhibition at the Tate Modern, opening Friday, brings together 80 works by South Korean artist Lee Bul spanning four decades. The retrospective includes her signature cyborg sculptures and large-scale architectural installations. Curator Achim Borchardt-Hume called Lee 'one of the most important sculptors working today.' The exhibition runs through January.
